title: practical english usage - Part 59
content:
You’ ve made the house beautiful.

The other children called her stupid.

Most people saw him  as a sort of clown.

We all regarded Kathy as an expert.

They elected Mrs Sanderson President.

Queen Victoria considered him  a genius.

a noun or adjective which  describes or classifies the object.

After some verbs the direct object can be followed by an 'object complement’  - 65 He  was     considered            a  genius      .  _________ what (hey-think;) but not We like our staff to say what they think, (           O ur staff are liked-to-say (he manager) but not Everybody wanted Doris to be the manager, (          Doris was wanted to be passive structures with infinitives after them.

Verbs that refer to wanting, liking and similar ideas cannot usually be used in 6 exceptions:       wanting      and   liking grammar •  67  When  do we  use passive structures?

For more about the way information is organised in sentences, ►    267.

For -ing forms with passive meanings after need and want (e.g. My watch needs cleaning), ►     100.3.

For more about reflexive verbs, ►     178.   For active and passive past participles, ►    96.3-96.4.

me - the phrase Emily... do would make a very long subject.) (More natural than Emily wanting to tell everybody what to do annoyed I was annoyed by Emily wanting to tell everybody what to do.

be a reason for choosing a passive structure.

Longer and heavier expressions often go at the end of a clause, and this can also 4 putting      heavier      expressions         at  the   end (More natural than He waited for two hours; then a doctor saw him ...) which he had to wait for another hour before he was allowed to go home.

getting angry. Then he was taken upstairs and examined by a specialist, after to the waiting room. He sat there for another two hours - by this time he was He waited for two hours; then he was seen by a doctor; then he was sent back switch from active to passive and back.

In order to keep talking about the same person or thing, it may be necessary to 3 keeping      the   same    subject the 'news’  - the painter - can go at the end) 'Nice picture.’ ’ Yes, it was painted by my grandmother.’ (passive verb so that can go at the end) Jack's painting my portrait. (active verb so that the ‘ news’  - the portrait - another common reason for choosing passive structures. Compare: that we  are already talking about, and to put the 'news’  at the end. This is We   often prefer to begin a sentence with something that is already known, or 2 putting      the   news    at  the   end The results have not yet been analysed.

Too many books have been written about the Second World War.

Those pyramids were built around 400 AD.

for this reason.

Passives without 'agents’  (►  58) are common in academic and scientific writing are not so interested in saying (or not able to say) who  or what does/did it.

We   often choose passive structures when we  want to talk about an action, but 1  interest      in  the   action 67 When    do   we   use   passive      structures?       ______ Section 6 Passives grammar •  Section 7  Modal  Auxiliary Verbs A female crocodile may lay 30-40 eggs. I used to play a lot of tennis.

Dan can really get on your nerves. Most evenings he'll just sit watching TV.

can, may, will, would and used to.

We   can talk about what people and things typically or habitually do or did, using 4    typical behaviour (►  86-87) She can speak six languages. My old car could do 120mph.

are able to do because of their knowledge, skill, design and so on.

Modal  verbs, especially can and could, are used to say what people and things 3     ability (►  82-85) Must you go and see Anna? (Do you think it is necessary?) You must  go and see Anna. (ɪ think it is necessary.) in statements, and from the hearer's in questions. Compare: Note that these meanings are usually expressed from the speaker's point of view If you will come this way...      Could I talk to you for a moment?

Students must  register by 30 January. You should see the doctor.

They  are common in requests, suggestions, invitations and instructions.
